title: Language models are multilingual chain-of-thought reasoners

structured.summary: This paper introduces the Multilingual Grade School Math (MGSM) benchmark for evaluating the reasoning abilities of large language models across multiple languages. It demonstrates that intermediate reasoning steps in English significantly enhance multilingual performance and reveals the models' strong reasoning abilities even in underrepresented languages.

strengths.evidence: The MGSM benchmark is a pioneering approach to evaluate arithmetic reasoning across ten typologically diverse languages.
The study shows that the 540-billion-parameter model achieved a 55% solve rate across languages, indicating robust multilingual reasoning capabilities.

weaknesses.point: Lack of cultural adaptation in translations.
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not address whether math problems may favor certain languages due to cultural contexts, impacting fairness across translated problems.
The evaluation section lacks an in-depth analysis of specific errors which would provide insights into the weaknesses of the models.

weaknesses.point: Fragmented clarity in presenting prompting strategies.
weaknesses.evidence: The discussion of different prompting strategies is spread across multiple sections, complicating comprehension of the experiment setup.

questions.question: How were cultural differences considered in the problem translations?
questions.motivation: Understanding whether translations consider cultural contexts is important for ensuring that the benchmark fairly assesses multilingual reasoning abilities.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of cultural adaptation in translations.. The paper does not address whether math problems may favor certain languages due to cultural contexts, impacting fairness across translated problems
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient error analysis.. The evaluation section lacks an in-depth analysis of specific errors which would provide insights into the weaknesses of the models
critique_points.point_003: Fragmented clarity in presenting prompting strategies.. The discussion of different prompting strategies is spread across multiple sections, complicating comprehension of the experiment setup
